title: Horror TV
subtitle: Slow-burn psychological, gothic, supernatural, anthology horror TV.

items.title: Buffy the Vampire Slayer
items.seasons: All 7 (ended 2003); 'Once More With Feeling' (S6E7) is among the great hours of TV
items.pitch: Whedon's teenage vampire-slayer ensemble. The structural template for genre TV that takes its characters seriously.

critics.quote: Routinely cited among the most influential genre TV ever made.

items.title: Hannibal
items.pitch: NBC. Bryan Fuller. Mads Mikkelsen as Hannibal Lecter; Hugh Dancy as Will Graham. Visually astonishing — gore as Renaissance-painting composition. The most stylized prestige horror on a major network.

critics.quote: Critical darling throughout. S2 is Fuller's structural peak. The cancellation by NBC remains one of the most-mourned in modern TV.

sections.name: III. High Priority
sections.desc: Strong fits given your slow-burn psychological horror loves (Witch, Lighthouse, Hereditary, Nosferatu 2024).

items.title: The Haunting of Hill House
items.whyPriority: Netflix. Mike Flanagan's breakthrough. Family-trauma horror dressed as ghost story. The single-take Episode 6 ('Two Storms') is a TV-history-grade structural achievement.
items.pitch: Five adult siblings reunite after their sister's death and confront the haunted house they grew up in.

critics.quote: Critical and commercial breakthrough for Flanagan.

items.title: Midnight Mass
items.whyPriority: Netflix. Flanagan's most personal work. Catholicism, addiction, vampires (yes, vampires). The Riley/Erin/Father Paul triangulation is among the great TV religious dramas.
items.pitch: A disgraced young man returns to his isolated island hometown. A new priest arrives. Strange miracles begin.

critics.quote: Considered Flanagan's most ambitious and most divisive (in length and dialogue density).

items.title: The Terror
items.seasons: S1 strongest; S2 (Infamy) divisive; S3 in development
items.whyPriority: AMC. S1 is one of the great modern horror-of-isolation pieces. The lost Franklin expedition + supernatural element. Direct fit for Eggers/Witch sensibility.
items.pitch: 1845. Two Royal Navy ships search for the Northwest Passage and become trapped in Arctic ice. Something is hunting them.

critics.quote: S1 critical darling; routinely cited among the great limited-series horror works of the late 2010s.

items.title: Penny Dreadful
items.seasons: All 3 (ended 2016); spin-off City of Angels (2020) is unrelated
items.whyPriority: Showtime. Victorian London, gothic literature monsters mash-up. Eva Green delivers one of the great horror TV performances. Direct fit for your vampire-and-Eggers lane.
items.pitch: Frankenstein, Dracula, Dorian Gray, and original characters intersect in Victorian London while a séance medium battles demonic forces.

critics.quote: Eva Green won the Globe and was Emmy-nominated for the lead. Cult devotion.

items.title: Channel Zero
items.whyPriority: SyFy. Anthology horror — each season adapts a different creepypasta. S2 (No-End House) considered the standout. Genuinely unsettling.
items.pitch: Each season a self-contained creepypasta-derived horror. Tooth-related, structure-based, dream-based.

critics.quote: Cult favorite. Quality varies by season; S2 widely considered the strongest.

items.title: Servant
items.whyPriority: Apple TV+. M. Night Shyamalan executive-produced. Slow-burn horror about a young couple, their nanny, and a doll that may not be a doll.
items.pitch: A grieving Philadelphia couple uses a reborn doll to help process their infant son's death. The new nanny treats the doll as alive. Then it isn't a doll.

items.title: Stranger Things
items.pitch: Netflix. 80s-set sci-fi/horror. The cultural phenomenon of mid-2010s genre TV.

critics.quote: Critical reputation has cooled across seasons; the cultural footprint remains enormous.

items.title: The Haunting of Bly Manor
items.seasons: Limited series; sequel to Hill House
items.pitch: Flanagan's second Haunting. Henry James adaptation. Less acclaimed than Hill House but much better-written than Netflix-horror-television generally is.

critics.quote: Critical reception softer than Hill House; defenders consider the romance arc strong.

items.title: Yellowjackets
items.whyPriority: Showtime. High school girls' soccer team plane crashes in Canadian wilderness in 1996. The show alternates between their survival and their adult lives 25 years later.
items.pitch: Survival horror with dual-timeline structure. The wilderness was supernatural. The adult lives are unraveling.

items.title: Inside No. 9
items.whyPriority: BBC. From the League of Gentlemen creators. Each episode a self-contained 30-minute story that twists. Consistently among the most inventive things on TV.
items.pitch: Each episode set in a place numbered 9. Otherwise the stories share nothing.

critics.quote: BAFTA-winning. Cult-to-classic standing. Routinely cited among the best British TV of the 2010s-2020s.

items.title: Black Mirror
items.pitch: Cross-listed Sci-Fi TV. Tech-paranoia anthology. Episode quality varies wildly.

items.title: Tales from the Loop
items.pitch: Amazon. Based on Simon Stålenhag's painting series. Sci-fi with horror-of-the-uncanny aesthetic.

critics.quote: Cult favorite. Visually closest TV has come to Stålenhag's paintings.

items.title: The X-Files
items.seasons: S1-S7 are the canonical run; S8-9 weakened; S10-11 revivals
items.pitch: Cross-listed Sci-Fi TV. Paranormal investigations + government conspiracy. Mostly sci-fi but plenty of horror episodes.

items.title: 30 Coins (30 Monedas)
items.pitch: HBO Spain. Catholic-mystical horror with a small-town priest investigating an ancient conspiracy. Maximalist.

critics.quote: Cult international hit. Polarizing because it goes everywhere.
